skill.name: vercel-deploy
skill.description: Deploy applications and websites to Vercel by packaging a project and sending it to a claimable deployment endpoint.
skill.summary: Package a web project, upload it for Vercel deployment, and return preview and claim URLs.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and blocker patterns. Most Markdown command and path findings are false positives, but the shell script intentionally packages project files and uploads them to an external deployment endpoint. The skill is publishable with a clear warning to review the deployment directory and remove secrets before use.

medium_findings.title: Project Archive Uploaded to External Deployment Endpoint
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the script defines a remote deployment endpoint and posts the selected project tarball to it. This is the skill purpose, but it can expose source code and local files if the user deploys a sensitive directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The curl POST includes the tarball as a form file to a hardcoded HTTPS endpoint. The network transfer is explicit and semantically central to deployment.

medium_findings.title: Deployment Package Does Not Exclude Common Secret Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE semantic risk: the tar command excludes node_modules and .git, but no evidence found that it excludes .env files, private keys, local databases, or other secret-bearing files. A user could accidentally upload secrets with the deployment package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exclusion list is visible on the tar command and is limited to two directory names. The impact depends on the user's project contents, so this is medium risk.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution Used for Deployment Utilities
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious execution: command substitutions read package.json, create a temp directory, resolve paths, inspect HTML files, run curl, and parse the response. The script still deserves caution because it processes a user-selected path and uploads the resulting archive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The substitutions are visible and support packaging and response parsing. I did not find evidence that user input is evaluated as shell code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: the backtick and bash snippets in the skill instructions show users how to run the deployment script. They are not executable code inside the skill file itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears in Markdown or TOON examples, not in an executed script. The actual executable behavior is contained in scripts/deploy.sh.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detector Was Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: no cryptographic operation or weak hash algorithm is present at the flagged documentation lines. The lines describe deployment behavior and packaging, not cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines contain plain deployment descriptions and command examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, encryption, signing, or password handling.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Link Triggered Path Traversal Pattern
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: the '..' sequence appears in a Markdown link to a related skill. It is not used by runtime code to read or write files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a static documentation link. I did not find evidence that this path is consumed by the deployment script.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Upload of User-Selected Project Contents
dangerous_patterns.description: The deployment flow sends a tarball from the selected path to a hardcoded external endpoint. This is intended behavior but should be disclosed before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The input path, archive creation, and upload call are present in the script. The destination is hardcoded and not user-confirmed in the script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Archive Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The package command archives the project root while excluding only node_modules and .git. Sensitive files outside those exclusions may be included.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tar command and exclusions are explicit. Risk depends on project contents, but the pattern is easy to verify.

content.value_statement: Deploying local web projects can require setup, accounts, and manual packaging. This skill packages a project, detects its framework, and returns Vercel preview and claim links.
actual_capabilities: Packages a selected project directory into a compressed deployment archive.
Excludes node_modules and .git from generated deployment packages.
Detects many JavaScript web frameworks from package.json dependencies.
Accepts an existing .tgz archive instead of creating a new package.
Renames one root HTML file to index.html for simple static sites.
Returns preview and claim URLs from the deployment response.
limitations: Uploads the selected project archive to an external deployment endpoint.
Does not automatically exclude .env files, keys, databases, or other secrets.
Requires shell tools, curl, tar, grep, and network access to the endpoint.
Claiming the deployment still requires the user to open the claim URL.

use_cases.title: Share a Preview Build
use_cases.description: Deploy a working branch and send a Vercel preview URL for review.

use_cases.title: Publish a Static Demo
use_cases.description: Turn a small HTML project into a hosted page without manual Vercel setup.

use_cases.title: Transfer a Deployment
use_cases.description: Create a claimable deployment that can be moved into a Vercel account.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy Current Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner deployment from the active folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy this web app to Vercel and show me the preview URL and claim URL.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a Specific Folder
prompt_templates.scenario: Deploy when the project is not the active folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy the project in this folder to Vercel. Confirm the folder first, then return both deployment links.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a Static HTML Demo
prompt_templates.scenario: Publish a simple site without package.json
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy this static HTML demo to Vercel. If it has one HTML file, make it work from the root URL.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Safe Preview Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the deployment folder for secrets, remove files that should not be uploaded, then deploy a Vercel preview.

output_examples.output: Deployment complete. The response includes a live preview URL and a claim URL for transferring ownership.
The folder is packaged, the root HTML entry is prepared, and the deployment links are returned.

output_examples.input: The deployment failed because network access is blocked.
output_examples.output: The result explains that network egress is required and points the user to allow Vercel domains before retrying.

best_practices: Deploy only a clean project directory that has been reviewed for secrets.
Show both the preview URL and claim URL after every successful deployment.
Use the generated preview URL to validate the site before sharing it widely.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy a home directory, workspace root, or repository that contains private files.
Do not treat the preview link as private access control.
Do not ignore deployment errors or hide missing network access from the user.

faq.question: Does this skill require Vercel authentication?
faq.answer: The script uses a claimable deployment flow. The user needs the claim URL to transfer the deployment.

faq.question: What files are excluded from the package?
faq.answer: The script excludes node_modules and .git. Users should remove secrets and other private files before deployment.

faq.question: Which frameworks can it detect?
faq.answer: It checks package.json for many common JavaScript frameworks, including Next.js, Gatsby, Remix, Vue, Svelte, Astro, and Express.

faq.question: Can it deploy a plain HTML site?
faq.answer: Yes. If there is one root HTML file and no package.json, it can rename that file to index.html.

faq.question: What happens if network access is blocked?
faq.answer: The deployment fails. The user must allow access to the required Vercel deployment domains and try again.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The selected project archive is uploaded externally, so private or secret files should be removed first.
